name: Boron
name_origin: Named from 'borax' (the mineral from which it was first isolated) plus the suffix '-on' suggested by analogy with carbon.

discovery.discoverer: Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac, Louis Jacques Thenard, and (independently) Humphry Davy

occurrence.abundance_summary: Boron is relatively rare in Earth's crust (~10 ppm) and not found as the free element. Major deposits are evaporite borate minerals (borax, kernite, ulexite, colemanite), with the largest reserves in Turkey and California.
states: in borate evaporite minerals (borax, kernite, colemanite)
dissolved in some hot springs and brines
trace nutrient in plants and soils

compounds.name: Borax (sodium tetraborate decahydrate)
compounds.summary: The most familiar boron compound, used as a cleaning agent, flux for soldering, and feedstock for other boron chemicals.

compounds.name: Boric acid
compounds.summary: A weak acid used as a mild antiseptic, insecticide, and neutron absorber in nuclear reactor coolant.

compounds.name: Boron trioxide
compounds.summary: The primary oxide of boron, a key flux component in borosilicate glass and ceramic glazes.

compounds.name: Boron nitride
compounds.summary: Exists in graphite-like (hexagonal) and diamond-like (cubic) forms; cubic BN is second only to diamond in hardness and used as an abrasive.

compounds.name: Decaborane
compounds.summary: A cluster compound studied as a high-energy fuel and a precursor for carborane synthesis.

compounds.name: Boron trifluoride
compounds.summary: A pungent gas and powerful Lewis acid widely used as a catalyst in organic synthesis.

uses: Borosilicate glass (Pyrex) for laboratory glassware and cookware
Fiberglass insulation and reinforcement (the largest single use)
Boron-10 enriched control rods and coolant additive in nuclear reactors
Boron carbide armor and abrasive grit
Doping agent for p-type semiconductors in silicon electronics
reactivity_summary: Elemental boron is a hard, brittle metalloid that is chemically inert at room temperature but reacts at elevated temperatures with oxygen, halogens, and many metals to form borides. Its chemistry is dominated by electron-deficient covalent bonding, leading to rich cluster chemistry (boranes, carboranes) found in no other element except carbon.
isotopes_summary: Boron has two stable isotopes: 11B (~80.1%) and 10B (~19.9%); natural variability in the ratio gives an interval atomic weight. 10B has an exceptionally large neutron-capture cross section, making it valuable for nuclear reactor control and boron neutron capture therapy (BNCT) for cancer.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Boron
